title: Recursion
description: Recursion appears in tree traversals, backtracking, divide-and-conquer, and dynamic programming. Master the base case pattern, memoization with Map, and converting between recursive and iterative approaches.

sections.title: Base case pattern
sections.explanation: Every recursive function needs a base case to stop recursion and a recursive case that reduces the problem. Forgetting the base case causes infinite recursion and a stack overflow. In interviews, always define the base case first — it clarifies your thinking and prevents bugs.

exercise.prompt: Write a recursive function `sumTo(n)` that returns the sum from 1 to n. Base case: n <= 0 returns 0. Print sumTo(5) and sumTo(10).

validation.message: Use a recursive call to sumTo with a decreasing argument

exercise.hint: Base case returns 0. Recursive case: n + sumTo(n - 1).

sections.title: Memoization with Map
sections.explanation: Memoization caches results of expensive recursive calls to avoid redundant computation. Use a Map to store computed results keyed by the input. This turns exponential-time problems (like naive Fibonacci) into O(n) time. In interviews, memoization is the bridge from brute-force recursion to dynamic programming.

exercise.prompt: Implement memoized Fibonacci. Use a Map for the cache. Print fib(10) and fib(20).

validation.message: Use Map's has() to check cache and set() to store results

exercise.hint: Use memo.has(n) to check cache, memo.set(n, result) to store.

sections.title: Backtracking pattern
sections.explanation: Backtracking builds solutions incrementally, abandoning a path when it can't lead to a valid solution. The pattern is: choose, explore (recurse), unchoose (backtrack). This applies to permutations, combinations, N-Queens, and Sudoku. In JavaScript, push/pop on an array is the idiomatic choose/unchoose.

exercise.prompt: Generate all subsets of [1, 2, 3] using backtracking. Print each subset as a comma-joined string (empty string for empty set), one per line, sorted by length then values.

validation.message: All 8 subsets of [1,2,3] sorted by size then lexicographic
Use pop() to backtrack and recursive calls to explore

exercise.hint: pop() undoes the choice. Start backtrack at index 0.

sections.title: Converting recursion to iteration
sections.explanation: Some interviewers ask you to convert recursive solutions to iterative ones. Use an explicit stack to simulate the call stack. Push parameters onto the stack instead of making recursive calls. This avoids stack overflow for deep recursion and demonstrates strong algorithm understanding.

exercise.prompt: Convert recursive factorial to iterative using a loop. Print the iterative factorial of 5 and 7.

validation.message: Use a for loop to iteratively compute the factorial

exercise.hint: Start i at 2 (or 1) and multiply: result *= i.
